Retrieved Evidence:
[1] World Health Organization (WHO)
The World Health Organization (WHO) defines child mortality as the number of deaths of children under five years of age per 1,000 live births. This metric is widely used internationally to assess progress toward achieving Sustainable Development Goal 3 – Good Health and Well-being. [1]
[2] UNICEF & WHO Joint Report on Maternal and Child Health
UNICEF and WHO jointly emphasize that measuring child mortality requires tracking deaths among children aged zero to four years inclusive. However, due to practical limitations in data collection, many countries report estimates based on the 0-to-5-year-old cohort. [2]
[3] Global Burden of Disease Study
The Global Burden of Disease Study consistently reports child mortality figures derived from deaths among children aged 0 to 4 years, citing methodological consistency with existing databases and historical trends in mortality patterns. [3]
[4] WHO Guidelines on Neonatal Care
Neonatal mortality specifically refers to deaths occurring within the first 28 days of life. While part of the broader child mortality picture, it does not replace the 0–5 year benchmark. [4]
[5] WHO Framework for Monitoring Child Survival Indicators
The WHO recommends using the age group 0–5 years for calculating child mortality indicators, noting that this range best captures the full scope of preventable causes affecting young children—from congenital anomalies to malnutrition and infectious diseases. [5]
[6] Data Sources Used in National Surveys
National surveys commonly collect demographic data on children aged 0–5 years to estimate mortality rates accurately, especially since younger children represent the largest proportion of deaths in early development stages. [6]
[7] Definitions in Public Health Literature
Public health literature broadly accepts that "children under five" equates to those aged 0–5 years when discussing mortality statistics. This usage ensures comparability across studies worldwide. [7]
[8] Methodological Consistency Across Studies
Using a uniform age threshold enables researchers and policymakers to compare findings across regions and timeframes reliably. Deviations from 0–5 could introduce inconsistencies in long-term trend analyses. [8]
